title: Cat Pooping Outside the Litter Box: Causes and What to Do
meta_desc: Is your cat pooping outside the box? Learn why this happens, the urgency level, and how sharing photos with your vet can help find a solution.
content_html:
TL;DR: Cats pooping outside the box usually indicate medical discomfort or environmental issues; while typically not an emergency, seek immediate care if your cat is straining, vomiting, or lethargic.

What does it mean when my cat poops outside the litter box?

Inappropriate defecation occurs when a cat deposits stool in areas like the floor or furniture instead of their litter box. This behavior is usually a signal that something is wrong, ranging from medical discomfort to dissatisfaction with their environment or the cleanliness of their box.

When should I worry if my cat is pooping outside the box?

While frustrating, this is rarely a life-threatening emergency. You should schedule a routine veterinary appointment, but contact an emergency clinic if your cat shows:

  • Extreme lethargy
  • Constant vomiting
  • Inability to pass stool despite straining

How can a photo of the stool help my veterinarian diagnose the problem?

Providing your veterinarian with a photo of the stool and its location is incredibly diagnostic, as it allows the vet to evaluate:

  • The consistency (checking for diarrhea or constipation) and color
  • The presence of any blood or mucus
  • If the location suggests mobility issues, such as arthritis, or a specific aversion to the litter box area

Clinical Context (Merck Veterinary Manual)

If medical problems have been excluded or treated, focus on the behavioral history to determine the cause of inappropriate elimination. Avoidance may arise from issues with the litter substrate (texture, depth, scent, cleanliness), box (size, shape, hood), or location that reduce appeal, or unpleasant experiences at or near the box (e.g., insufficient cleaning, noises, pain). Difficulty accessing the box or conflict between cats in the household can also be factors. Treatment should focus on providing an appealing litter, box, and location; reducing or preventing access to the soiled location; and resolving underlying contributing factors. Identify and resolve potential deterrents such as undesirable locations, fear-evoking stimuli, or limited access. Improving access (e.g., larger box, ease of entry/exit) and determining the cat's preferred litter (e.g., sand, clumping, clay, scented) and box type (e.g., size, shape, height, covered) by offering choices is important. A thorough behavioral history should include details about whether the elimination is urine or feces, if urine deposits are vertical (spraying) or horizontal (soiling), duration and frequency of the problem, signalment and temperament of the cat, when and where the cat eliminates, litter box details (number, placement, cleaning, substrate, size), the cat's daily routine, and its home environment.

Chapter: Behavioral

Source: The Merck Veterinary Manual, 11th Edition (Page 1573)

faqs.question: Is my cat pooping outside the box out of spite?
faqs.answer: No, cats do not act out of spite. This behavior is typically a response to physical discomfort, stress, or a problem with the litter box itself.

faqs.question: Could my cat have a medical problem?
faqs.answer: Yes, conditions such as intestinal parasites, inflammatory bowel disease (IBD), or constipation can make the act of defecating uncomfortable, leading the cat to associate the box with pain.

faqs.question: What is the 'Golden Rule' for litter boxes?
faqs.answer: Veterinarians recommend having one more litter box than the number of cats in your home (e.g., two boxes for one cat) to prevent behavioral issues.
